title: Hasselback Potatoes
subtitle: Fan-cut, herb-buttered, golden-edged potatoes
excerpt: Hasselback potatoes are whole potatoes cut into thin slices that open like a fan during roasting, giving a crisp exterior and a tender interior in a single potato. The slicing is the work; the oven does the rest. Herb butter worked into the cuts as they open during cooking gives each slice its own basted edge.
sourceNotes: The Hasselback potato originates from the Hasselbacken restaurant in Stockholm, Sweden, where the technique was developed in the 1950s. Now a standard part of British home cooking. Method adapted from traditional Swedish preparation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A series of parallel cuts made from top to bottom through a whole potato, stopping 5 mm short of the base so the potato stays in one piece. The cuts open like a fan during roasting, increasing the crisp surface area.

content.text: transforms a standard roasting potato into something with a dramatically larger surface area: every exposed edge crisps in the oven, and the gaps between slices fill with herb butter. The trick of stopping the knife 5 mm from the base is easier with a chopstick laid on either side of the potato to prevent cutting all the way through.
Medium-sized waxy or all-purpose potatoes work better here than large floury ones: they hold their shape through an hour of roasting without collapsing. Charlotte or Desiree varieties are ideal.

items.prepNote: medium-sized, approx. 150 g each, scrubbed (skin on)

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Place a potato on a board with a chopstick or wooden spoon along each side. Slice down at 3 mm intervals across the potato, stopping when the knife touches the chopsticks. The base holds the potato together. Repeat with all 8 {{potato}} potatoes.

Mix {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, the grated {{garlic}} of garlic, and thyme leaves. Brush generously over the cut potatoes, working the mixture into the cuts. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Place cut-side up in a roasting pan. Roast for 30 minutes.

By 30 minutes the cuts will have opened into a fan shape. Brush again with any remaining butter mixture, working it into the widened gaps. Return to the oven for a further 25 to 30 minutes until the edges are crisp and golden-brown and the interior is tender when tested with a skewer.

rows.symptom: Potato cut all the way through
rows.cause: No chopstick guide used or knife went past the guide
rows.fix: Not a problem: the potato becomes potato slices. It will still roast well, just not as a fan.

rows.cause: Cuts spaced too far apart (more than 5 mm)
rows.fix: Make cuts at 3 mm intervals. Wider cuts do not expand enough to fan out.

rows.symptom: Outside crisp but centre still raw at 60 minutes
rows.cause: Potato is very large or particularly dense
rows.fix: Continue roasting. A 200 g potato may need 75 minutes total.

content.text: The technique came from the Hasselbacken hotel in Stockholm in the 1950s and was popularised in British food writing during the 1980s and 1990s. It sits naturally alongside a roast as an alternative to plain roast potatoes when a dinner party presentation is wanted, or as a weeknight side that looks more deliberate than it is.
